Paul begins this chapter not with an explanation but with a prisoner's claim. He is bound in chains, yet he speaks of stewardship and mystery and grace as though his imprisonment itself proves the reality of what he proclaims. This is the weight of Ephesians 3: the gospel's power is not measured by comfort or safety, but by a man willing to suffer for it. The mystery Paul guards—that Gentiles are now fellow heirs with Jews in God's family—was hidden for ages, concealed in God's counsel before creation itself. What the prophets could not see, what generations of believers could not have predicted, now stands revealed through the Spirit to the apostles and prophets. God did not gradually inch toward this truth. He exploded it into history through the gospel, fundamentally reordering how His people understand His purposes. And Paul, who considers himself less than the least of all saints, has been entrusted with making this mystery visible to the world. His sufferings are not a setback to the gospel's credibility—they are its seal.

The prayer that follows reaches toward something almost beyond speech. Paul does not pray for ease or vindication. He prays that believers would be strengthened not in circumstance but in their inner being, that Christ would dwell so deeply in their hearts through faith that they would, together with all the saints, grasp something that surpasses knowledge—the love of Christ. This is not sentimental. To comprehend length and width and height and depth is to try to measure what cannot be measured, to know what cannot be fully known. Paul is asking God to fill His people with a reality so vast, so profound, that it transforms understanding itself. He prays this not as private devotion but as the proclamation of what God can actually do: the purpose God established before time began, He has accomplished in Christ. Every wall between Jew and Gentile, every partition between heaven and earth, every barrier between a sinful person and the Father's presence has been torn down through the cross and resurrection.

What makes this chapter dangerous—and it is dangerous—is that Paul expects this truth to be lived, not merely believed. The church exists so that the manifold wisdom of God becomes visible to authorities in the heavenly realms. Rulers and powers watch how God's people love one another across ancient divides, how Gentiles and Jews share one table, one Spirit, one Lord. The church's unity displays God's genius in a way no individual saint can. This is why Paul asks believers not to be discouraged by his sufferings; they are your glory. The cost of the gospel is not a tragedy to pity. It is the sign that someone is willing to stake their whole existence on the truth that Christ is worth more than freedom, more than safety, more than life itself. When you encounter that, when you see someone bearing Christ's name into imprisonment for the sake of His body, you are encountering not defeat but the vindication of everything Paul has written.

The chapter ends with Paul's doxology—not a question mark but an affirmation. To Him who is able to do immeasurably more than all we ask or imagine, according to His power at work within us, be the glory. This is the voice of a man in chains, declaring that God's power is not theoretical but active in the present moment, within us. Paul does not wait for heaven to celebrate God's capacity. He celebrates it now, knowing that Christ dwells in hearts through faith now, that the mystery continues to unfold now, that the church's witness continues to shine now. The gospel is not a promise about the future alone. It is the reality that changes who you are and what you can do, beginning today.
